project_and_lead_agency_information.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.cross_streets: West River Road and County Road 18
project_location.within_2_miles_schools: Riverbend High School; Riverbend Middle School; Riverbend Elementary School

development_type.transportation_type: New internal public streets, traffic circle at West River Road/Project Entry, and Class II/III bicycle lanes and multi-use trails
development_type.other_development_type: Neighborhood park (6.5 acres), greenway and riparian setback open space (6.5 acres), and community center (12,000 sq ft).

project_issues_discussed_in_document.other_project_issues: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions; environmental justice and community health considerations; construction-related vibration impacts.

existing_land_use_and_project_descriptio.present_land_use_zoning_general_plan_des: Present Land Use: Primarily irrigated row-crop agriculture (alfalfa and tomatoes) with a single rural residence and associated outbuildings on the northwest corner; remainder of site is generally vacant farmland with a private farm access road and irrigation infrastructure. Zoning: A-2 (General Agriculture) and a small portion zoned RR (Rural Residential) along West River Road frontage. General Plan Designation: Agriculture (AG) with a portion designated Rural Residential (RR). Surrounding land uses include the Sacramento River and riparian corridor to the west, existing single-family neighborhoods to the east across County Road 18, and agricultural uses to the north and south.
existing_land_use_and_project_descriptio.project_description:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is a 48.7-acre master-planned community proposed on largely agricultural land at the western edge of the City of Riverbend, immediately east of the Sacramento River and west of existing single-family neighborhoods. The project is being processed by the City of Riverbend Community Development Department on behalf of the applicant, Riverbend Village Partners, LLC.

The project would establish a compact, walkable mixed-use neighborhood that includes residential, neighborhood-serving commercial, office, civic, and recreational uses integrated with a system of parks, trails, and open space. The intent is to create a village-scale development that provides needed housing options and local services for existing and future Riverbend residents while respecting the adjacent Sacramento River corridor and surrounding agricultural context.

Major components of the project include:

1. Residential Development (312 units total)
- Up to 312 residential units in a mix of housing types, including:
  • Approximately 120 single-family detached homes on small lots (3,500–5,000 sq ft), primarily located along the eastern and southern portions of the site.
  • Approximately 96 single-family attached townhomes clustered near the neighborhood commercial center to support walkability.
  • Approximately 96 multi-family units in three-story garden-style buildings located adjacent to the village center and primary transit stops.
- Residential densities would range from roughly 6 to 18 dwelling units per acre, with an overall average density of about 11.8 units per residential acre.
- A minimum of 10% of the units (approximately 31 units) are proposed to be deed-restricted affordable housing, targeted to low- and moderate-income households, with a preference program for local workers and seniors where legally permissible.

2. Neighborhood Commercial and Office Uses
- Approximately 65,000 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial space concentrated in a village main street configuration near the primary entrance from West River Road. Anticipated uses include a small-format grocery store (approximately 25,000 square feet), neighborhood restaurants and cafés, personal services (e.g., barber, dry cleaner, fitness studio), and small retail shops.
- Approximately 45,000 square feet of office space in two- and three-story buildings intended to accommodate medical offices, professional services, co-working spaces, and small local businesses. The office component is estimated to support approximately 180 on-site employees at full occupancy.
- Building heights for commercial and office structures would generally range from one to three stories, with architectural design guidelines emphasizing pedestrian orientation, active frontages, and compatibility with nearby residential uses.

3. Parks, Open Space, and Community Facilities
- A 6.5-acre centrally located neighborhood park featuring multi-use turf fields, children’s play areas, picnic shelters, a half-court basketball area, outdoor fitness stations, and restroom facilities. The park is intended to serve both project residents and the surrounding community.
- Approximately 6.5 acres of greenway and open space along the western project edge, providing a setback from the Sacramento River and its riparian corridor. This area would include native planting, informal trails, interpretive signage about river ecology and local history, and seating/view areas. Portions of this area would remain in a more naturalized state to support habitat values.
- A 12,000-square-foot community center building near the village core, which may include a multi-purpose meeting hall, flexible classroom space for community programs, and offices for homeowners’ association or property management staff. The community center is envisioned as a venue for neighborhood events, classes, and civic meetings.
- An internal network of sidewalks, Class II and Class III bike facilities, and multi-use trails connecting residential neighborhoods to the village center, park, and river greenway. The project would tie into existing and planned regional trail systems where feasible.

4. Circulation and Access
- Primary vehicular access would be provided via a new signalized intersection on West River Road, with a landscaped median and dedicated left- and right-turn lanes. A secondary access point would be provided on County Road 18 for emergency access and local circulation.
- The project includes construction of new public local and collector streets within the site, designed with traffic calming features such as curb extensions, on-street parking, street trees, and enhanced crosswalks near the park and commercial areas.
- Bicycle and pedestrian improvements include buffered bike lanes along the main internal spine street, shared-lane markings (sharrows) on lower-speed residential streets, and off-street multi-use trails along the greenway. Bicycle parking would be provided at the park, community center, and commercial buildings.
- The project would coordinate with the local transit provider to install bus stops and shelters along West River Road and potentially within the village center to support expanded transit service in the future.

5. Infrastructure and Utilities
- Water service would be provided by the City of Riverbend’s municipal water system via extension of existing mains in County Road 18 and West River Road. Looped water lines within the project would provide fire flow and domestic service. The project would incorporate water-efficient fixtures and drought-tolerant landscaping.
- Wastewater would be conveyed via new gravity sewer lines to existing trunk mains in County Road 18, ultimately discharging to the City’s wastewater treatment plant. A detailed sewer capacity analysis has been prepared as part of the EIR to confirm available capacity and identify any off-site improvements.
- Stormwater would be managed using a combination of on-site bioretention facilities, vegetated swales, and underground detention where necessary. The project is designed to meet current low-impact development (LID) standards and to ensure post-development peak flows do not exceed pre-development conditions. Water quality treatment is provided prior to discharge to existing drainage channels that flow toward the Sacramento River.
- Electric and natural gas service would be provided by the regional utility provider via extension of nearby distribution lines. The project would be designed to accommodate rooftop solar on residential and commercial structures, electric vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure at multi-family and commercial parking areas, and energy-efficient building envelopes consistent with current Title 24 standards.

6. Phasing
- The project is anticipated to be built in three primary phases over approximately 8–10 years, depending on market conditions:
  • Phase 1: Infrastructure backbone (primary roads, utilities), initial single-family residential blocks, a portion of the neighborhood park, and a portion of the commercial village center.
  • Phase 2: Remaining single-family and townhome neighborhoods, completion of the neighborhood park, initial office buildings, and construction of the community center.
  • Phase 3: Multi-family residential buildings, remaining office and commercial space, and full build-out of the river greenway trails and open space improvements.

7. Entitlements Requested
- The project requires multiple discretionary approvals from the City of Riverbend, including: (1) General Plan Amendment to redesignate the site from Agriculture (AG) and Rural Residential (RR) to Mixed Use (MU) and Parks/Open Space (P/OS); (2) adoption of a Specific Plan establishing land use designations, development standards, and design guidelines for the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village; (3) Rezone from A-2 and RR to a combination of Mixed Use (MU-Village), Residential (R-1 and R-2), and Parks/Open Space (P/OS) zoning districts; (4) approval of a Planned Unit Development (PUD) overlay to allow flexible development standards and clustering; (5) approval of a Tentative Subdivision Map for land division into residential, commercial, office, park, and open space parcels and public street rights-of-way; (6) Site Plan and Design Review for the initial phases of development; (7) a Conditional Use Permit (Use Permit) for certain commercial uses (e.g., drive-through restaurant and community-serving uses) as identified in the zoning code; and (8) approval of a Development Agreement establishing phasing, infrastructure financing, and community benefit obligations.

8. Project Objectives
Key project objectives include: (1) providing a range of housing types and affordability levels to help address the City’s Regional Housing Needs Allocation; (2) creating a walkable, mixed-use environment that reduces vehicle miles traveled relative to conventional, single-use suburban development; (3) establishing a neighborhood commercial center that offers day-to-day goods and services to existing and future residents of west Riverbend; (4) enhancing public access to and appreciation of the Sacramento River and its riparian corridor while protecting sensitive habitat; (5) implementing complete streets and multi-modal transportation improvements; and (6) ensuring that new development contributes fairly to the cost of needed infrastructure and public services without unduly burdening existing residents.

The Draft Environmental Impact Report (EIR) analyzes the potential environmental effects of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project, including but not limited to impacts related to land use and planning, population and housing, transportation and circulation, air quality and greenhouse gas emissions, noise, biological and cultural resources, hydrology and water quality, public services and utilities, and cumulative and growth-inducing effects. The EIR also evaluates project alternatives, including a No Project Alternative, a Reduced Density Alternative, and an Increased River Setback Alternative, in order to identify environmentally superior options while still meeting most of the project’s basic objectives.
